input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous, teleoperated vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware architecture consists of lower‑level and upper‑level chassis structures together with autonomous‑control elements (e.g., perception sensors, actuators, and resource‑constrained electronic control units). The vehicle operates in a test‑bed environment for adaptive cruise control (ACC) research, providing a realistic yet manageable platform where vehicle dynamics, sensor streams, and actuator commands can be exercised and monitored, making it well suited for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) presents real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks; it also enables operators to view, save, and generate reports of the collected data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed set‑point calculation) is executed on a cloud‑based DT rather than on‑board ECUs; the DT returns the computed desired speed to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – through a low‑latency bidirectional link, operators can remotely enable or disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue emergency‑braking commands. These services support two user classes: operators (monitoring and control) and developers (troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires a shared clock across devices with a maximum synchronization error of 100 ms, as mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards; this accuracy is the basis for computation off‑loading between the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin is structured according to ISO 23247 as three top‑level entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on separate hardware. Within each entity, functionality is decomposed into Functional Entities (FEs) that are realized as ROS2 packages containing one or more ROS2 nodes; the User Interface FE is split into visualization and control‑panel sub‑components, while Data Storage exists as an unaligned FE. This hierarchical, multi‑entity arrangement constitutes a single logical twin rather than multiple parallel instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture: software components are first identified, then mapped to Functional Entities and instantiated as ROS2 packages; ROS2 topics/services provide intra‑entity communication, while deployment of each entity on distinct compute platforms enables resource partitioning. The overall constellation coordinates the Physical Twin Entity (sensors, actuators, point‑cloud clustering), the Digital Twin Entity (data management, control logic), and the User Entity (3D visualization, control panel) through ROS2 middleware, satisfying the entity‑based coordination pattern defined by the standard.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: ISO 23247 does not prescribe specific communication protocols, allowing the DT to integrate with external systems using standards of choice. The document cites OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as an example that can be employed for the Data Translation Functional Entity, indicating that the DT can expose or consume data via OPC UA to interoperate with enterprise IT, other Digital Twins, or external manufacturing execution systems. No additional integration patterns or external dependencies are described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin—including LiDAR point‑cloud streams, motor status, steering and servo positions, and overall vehicle state—is transmitted to the Digital Twin Entity. The raw point‑cloud is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (Data Pre‑Processing FE) which converts the 2‑D representation of individual points into clustered object tracks. All data streams are then passed to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE which serialises the messages, aligns their types and performs unit conversions. Communication between software components inside the DT uses a ROS 2 middleware network, while inter‑entity communication (physical twin ↔ digital twin) uses a Wi‑Fi link transport‑layer UDP. The system continuously streams the sensor data in real‑time (event‑driven by sensor output) to support the 3‑D visualization and ACC monitoring services.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin sends control commands back to the vehicle through the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel. Commands generated by the off‑loaded ACC service include a desired speed set‑point, an enable/disable ACC flag, and an emergency‑brake command. These commands are formatted by the Data Translation FE to match the vehicle's actuation message schema before transmission. Upon receipt, the vehicle’s control electronics execute the command (e.g., adjust motor speed or trigger braking) and provide implicit feedback via updated vehicle‑state messages; explicit acknowledgement is not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT system relies on two layers of networking. Internally, all software components communicate over a ROS 2 publish‑subscribe network. For communication between the physical twin and the Digital Twin Entity, a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer is employed. The architecture is designed for low end‑to‑end latency to satisfy safety‑critical ACC requirements, though exact bandwidth or latency figures are not given. No specific redundancy, reliability mechanisms, or security protocols (e.g., encryption, authentication) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The documents only state that the ACC service can be off‑loaded to “better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud,” implying a cloud‑based hosting option, but they provide no details on the concrete hosting platform, compute or storage sizing, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The ISO 23247 standard defines a development framework for digital twins divided into four parts (overview and general principles, reference architecture, digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and information exchange). It specifies an entity‑based reference architecture comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity, each built from Functional Entities that perform tasks such as actuation, data transformation, or user interfacing. The document does not provide further details on engineering methodologies, quality‑assurance practices, testing and validation procedures, version‑control mechanisms, or strategies for managing the evolution of a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The User Entity provides interfaces (e.g., 3‑D visualization and control panel) that allow operators to monitor live sensor data and issue control commands to the OME through the Digital Twin Entity. The DT implements three services: (1) Data Monitoring – continuous acquisition and presentation of OME state, (2) Computation Offloading – execution of analytics or simulation workloads on edge/cloud resources and feeding the results back to the user, and (3) Remote Management – remote configuration and actuation of the OME. These services generate actionable insights such as current operational status, predictive or diagnostic outcomes from offloaded computation, and enable decision making by allowing users to intervene based on the displayed information. No explicit confidence metrics are described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system follows the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing (2021). It adheres to Part 2 (Reference Architecture) which defines an entity‑based model comprising Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each built from Functional Entities. Information exchange conforms to the ISO 23247 Part 4 specifications. The implementation may employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for communication between the Data Collection and Device Control Entity and the OME, ensuring interoperability with industrial automation standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the physical twin as a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle built off a teleoperated Traxxas vehicle, retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. It correctly lists the main components (lower/upper chassis, sensors like LiDAR and Depth Camera, and computing units like Jetson NX/Orin Nano). The operational domain as a test-bed for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) is supported, as is the rationale for its suitability (resource-constrained ECUs, need for computation offloading). The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the text.

The extraction accurately captures the three core services described in the text: (1) Data Monitoring via UI to view/save/generate reports of vehicle state, paths, and tracks; (2) Computation Offloading of ACC logic (lead vehicle detection, speed calculation) to the cloud-based DT; and (3) Remote Management enabling ACC control, speed setting, and emergency braking via low-latency link. It correctly identifies the two user classes (operators and developers). The extraction is detailed, specific, and well-supported by the sources.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' but the Source Documents provide extensive details on tools, technologies, and frameworks used, including ISO 23247 RA, NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, ROS2, Python, C++, MATLAB/Simulink, Hokuyo LiDAR, Intel Depth Camera, and others. Since relevant information exists in the text, the extraction is incorrect.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: Correctly identifies the 100 ms synchronization accuracy and its link to computation offloading, though slightly misattributes the origin (text states standards mandate stricter limits, but the system requires 100 ms due to hardware). Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

Excellent extraction detailing the three entities (Physical, Digital, User), their deployment on separate hardware, the ROS2 package implementation, and the specific decomposition of the User Interface FE and unaligned Data Storage. Supported by quotes: 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', and 'User Interface FE has been split into two sub-components: visualization and control panel'.

Highly accurate description of the orchestration flow from software components to FEs to ROS2 packages, correctly mapping functionalities to the Physical Twin Entity (sensors, point-cloud clustering), Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Supported by quotes: 'First, we identify key software (SW) components... map the SW components onto FEs', 'Physical Twin Entity... sensors, actuators... Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking', and 'User Entity... 3D Visualization, Control Panel'.

Accurately reflects the lack of prescribed protocols in ISO 23247 and the specific mention of OPC UA. Supported by quotes: 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented' and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)... to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'. Correctly states no other integration patterns are described.

Data Pre-Processing FE', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network', 'Data Translation component... serializes data, aligns message types'.

It correctly identifies the absence of specific bandwidth figures, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols in the documents. II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)

_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework

representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is

It does not mention data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance regulations, consent management, or data anonymization.
